coredata.dc:title: Durable hydrophobic Nickel foams via two-step immersion: Stability, corrosion resistance, and oil–water separation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Baxevani, Aikaterini
Lamprou, Eleni
Stergioudi, Fani
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a two-step immersion method to produce a superhydrophobic film on nickel foams, for oil-water separation applications. The first step involves growing a silver layer to enhance surface micro-roughness, followed by a reduction of the surface energy using stearic acid in the second step. UV-Vis spectroscopy was used to optimize deposition times and monitor any possible film detachment during synthesis. The coating’s structure and elemental composition were characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Under optimal conditions, a strongly attached hydrophobic silver coating with a water contact angle (WCA) of ∼147° was successfully formed. Thermal stability tests showed that the WCA remained above 135° at −15 °C and above 140° at 100 °C, after 50 h of exposure, confirming robust surface durability. The coated foams achieved more than 95 % oil/water separation efficiency even after 15 cycles, demonstrating excellent reusability. A comparative evaluation of the corrosion behavior between the hydrophobic and the uncoated nickel foam was carried out using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a 3.5 % NaCl solution. The proposed two step immersion process for the creation of hydrophobic nickel foams proved to be efficient, cost-effective and adaptable.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Greece institutions: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel foam
Superhydrophobic coating
Corrosion
Oil-water separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the concentration of AgNO3 solution and immersion time
Thermal and chemical stability of the hydrophobic nickel foams

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process of hydrophobic nickel foam.

(a) WCA measurements for different immersion times in AgNO₃ solutions at various concentrations,F (b) Indicative UV–vis absorption spectrum, (c) nitrate ions’ absorbance as a function of time, (d) nitrate ions’ concentration as a function of time, (e) WCA measurements for different immersion times in stearic acid solution, (f) uncoated foam sinks and hydrophobic foam floats, (g) uncoated and reflective properties of hydrophobic foam.
SEM images of (a) uncoated nickel foam, (b-d) the morphologies developed on the hydrophobic nickel foam.

Contact angle measurements (a) after thermal treatment at 100 °C and −15 °C and (b) after chemical treatment in (b) HCl and NaOH solutions, (c) in distilled water and NaCl solution, (d) images of the samples after exposure.
(a) Tank for oil–water separation, (b) oil viscosities used, (c) separation efficiency for different types of oil after 15 filtration cycles.
(a) Oil absorption test using a hydrophobic nickel foam, (b) peristaltic pump, (c) oil-water separation using a peristaltic pump, (d) mass flow rate per filtration.
(a) Nyquist and (b) Bode curves for the uncoated and hydrophobic nickel foam, after immersion in sodium chloride (NaCl) solution.
Separation efficiency of modified nickel foams.

head.ce:title: Durable hydrophobic Nickel foams via two-step immersion: Stability, corrosion resistance, and oil–water separation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Metallurgy Laboratory, School of Mechanical Engineering, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki GR-54124, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physical Metallurgy Laboratory, School of Mechanical Engineering, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, GR-54124 Thessaloniki, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a two-step immersion method to produce a superhydrophobic film on nickel foams, for oil-water separation applications. The first step involves growing a silver layer to enhance surface micro-roughness, followed by a reduction of the surface energy using stearic acid in the second step. UV-Vis spectroscopy was used to optimize deposition times and monitor any possible film detachment during synthesis. The coating’s structure and elemental composition were characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Under optimal conditions, a strongly attached hydrophobic silver coating with a water contact angle (WCA) of ∼147° was successfully formed. Thermal stability tests showed that the WCA remained above 135° at −15 °C and above 140° at 100 °C, after 50 h of exposure, confirming robust surface durability. The coated foams achieved more than 95 % oil/water separation efficiency even after 15 cycles, demonstrating excellent reusability. A comparative evaluation of the corrosion behavior between the hydrophobic and the uncoated nickel foam was carried out using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a 3.5 % NaCl solution. The proposed two step immersion process for the creation of hydrophobic nickel foams proved to be efficient, cost-effective and adaptable.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Separating oil from water is an important research topic because oil spills are dangerous for the environment. Spills can be caused by many industries, like oil refining, metal processing, mining, textile and leather production and food factories [1,2]. They harm nature, people's health, and the economy. When water sources get polluted, especially in public areas, it creates serious health problems because some pollutants are hard to remove and stay in the environment for a long time [3,4]. The massive volume of oil-contaminated wastewater, including catastrophic spills like the Deepwater Horizon disaster, highlights the need for innovative, high-efficiency separation techniques [5–8].

Various porous materials are mentioned in the literature, such as metal meshes, textiles, sponges, membranes and foams, which are used as substrates for developing hydrophobic coatings aimed at oil-water separation applications [9–13]. Textiles are commonly used materials, however, their smaller surface area compared to metal foams affects their effectiveness in adsorption and separation applications [14–17]. Polyurethane (PU) foams lack high durability and may degrade over time, especially under harsh environmental conditions [18–22]. Another material used in oil-water separation applications is sponge. Although sponges are flexible materials that can take various shapes and adapt to different surface geometries, they have low durability and can deteriorate or lose their structural integrity after repeated use. Furthermore, since sponge-based separation relies on absorption, the need for frequent replacement or cleaning of the absorbent material makes the method less efficient and practical for applications involving large volumes of substances to be separated [23–26]. Metal meshes are also widely used for pollutants separation. However, their smaller surface area compared to foams limits their effectiveness in separation and adsorption applications [27,28]. Metal foams offer many advantages over other substrates, such as large surface area and durability. Hydrophobic coatings on foam substrates have proven to be highly effective for environmental and industrial applications. Hydrophobic foams have the advantage of absorbing large amounts of oil relative to their volume, making them suitable for use as filters in separating water from oils, aiming at cleaning seawater from pollutants [29].

The fabrication of hydrophobic surfaces typically involves two parameters: increasing the micro/nano roughness of the substrate and reducing its surface energy. These two factors are necessary to minimize the contact area between water droplets and the surface, leading to high water contact angles and effective water repellency [30–33]. Despite the variety of methods reported for producing superhydrophobic coatings, challenges related to their durability and long-term performance still remain. Many fabrication techniques involve complex, multi-step processes, expensive materials or time-consuming procedures. Kyoung Yong et al. used a mixture containing 60 wt% PTFE, 20 wt% R805 and 20 wt% PVDF (binder, 8 wt% dissolved in DMSO) was ball-milled for 30 min to form a slurry. This slurry was then used to coat nickel (Ni) foam. The Ni foam was immersed in the slurry for 1 min and then oven-dried at 115°C for 120 min to remove the solvent (DMSO). This process was repeated three times to ensure an even coating. The use of toxic solvents like DMSO and the repeated coating steps make this method time-consuming and environmentally challenging, particularly due to solvent removal [34]. Wei et. al used Ni foam as a substrate for growing ZnO nanostructures. The foam is first soaked in NaOH or HNO3 at 80°C for 1 h to remove surface contaminants, followed by ultrasonic cleaning. After drying, ZnO porous multilayer flower-like structures are grown using a hydrothermal process. This is followed by thermal treatment at 400°C for 2 h. The ZnO-coated foam is then subjected to hydrophobic treatment by dipping in a 1 wt% PFAS solution in hexane for 24 h at room temperature, followed by rinsing and drying. This method involves toxic chemicals (PFAS), making it hazardous, and it requires a long processing time, especially the 24-hour soaking in the PFAS solution—which slows down production [35]. Another research work, involves a process where a Ni foam was treated with a solution of Co(NO3)2, NH4F and urea in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 5 h at 120°C. After cooling, the foam is dried, then annealed at 800°C in an NH3 atmosphere for 4 h. Following this, the foam is treated with 1 H,1 H,2 H,2H-perfluorodecyltrimethoxysilane for 1 h at 60°C for hydrophobic coating. This method is extremely energy-intensive due to the high-temperature treatments and requires specialized equipment such as autoclaves and tubular furnaces, making it both time-consuming and costly [36]. These methods, have several drawbacks such as the multiple preparation steps, high temperatures, complex and energy-intensive processes, which are both time-consuming and costly, limiting their practical application for large-scale or environmentally-friendly production.

This study presents a simple method for fabricating hydrophobic nickel foams by reducing steps, avoiding the use of large amounts of energy and expensive equipment, and requiring only small amounts of chemicals. Its faster and simpler procedure makes it more affordable and easier to apply in practical applications. Furthermore, the reduced energy and chemical consumption enhances environmental friendliness and makes the method suitable for large-scale industrial use, where both cost and sustainability are critical. This approach has the potential to improve efficiency and reduce environmental impact, making it a promising option for future industrial and environmental applications. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to investigate the corrosion behavior of the hydrophobic nickel foam in a 3.5 % NaCl solution. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to examine the surface morphology. The oil-water separation efficiency of the produced foams, as well as their thermal and chemical durability, were also evaluated.

Silver nitrate with purity higher than 99.8 % was supplied by Panreac (Barcelona, Spain), while stearic acid (C18H36O2) with purity higher of 97 % was supplied by Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The nickel foams, which have a porosity of 95.2 %, are available in 10 cm × 10 cm × 1.6 mm sheets, were provided by Metafoam Technologies (Brossard, Canada). Ethanol was acquired from Merck KGaA, purity of 99 % (Darmstadt, Germany). Sodium hydroxide with purity of 98 % (NaOH) and a 37 % hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution, used in chemical stability tests, were obtained from Honeywell (Seelze, Germany) and Panreac (Barcelona, Spain), respectively.

Nickel foam sheets (15 mm × 2 mm) with a circular shape were cleaned with ultrasound in acetone and ethanol for 5 min, alternating between the two solvents three times, to remove surface impurities before further processing. Following the treatment, the nickel foams were submerged in a 20 mM ethanolic solution of silver nitrate (AgNO3) at 50 °C to enhance their micro/nano-scale roughness. Subsequently, they were immersed in a 15 mM ethanolic solution of stearic acid to lower their surface energy. The coated nickel foams were then rinsed with deionized water and left to dry at ambient temperature. Specifically, only minimal amounts of chemicals were needed in this process. Just 0.17 gr of silver nitrate and 0.21 gr of stearic acid were enough to coat four samples ( Fig. 1). These minimal quantities highlight the efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the method, as using such low amounts of materials reduces waste and makes the process more environmentally friendly and economical. This method for the development of a hydrophobic coating was previously applied to copper foam substrates, demonstrating its applicability to different metal foam substrates [37].

The surface morphology of the hydrophobic nickel foams, was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (Phenom ProX desktop SEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Eindhoven, The Netherlands). The microstructure and elemental composition of the superhydrophobic coatings were investigated by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Wettability was evaluated by measuring the static water contact angle, where 8 µL water droplets were deposited onto the surfaces of the samples. To assess the stability and long-term durability of hydrophobic nickel foams, a detailed study was performed to examine their behavior under various solutions and temperature conditions. Thermal treatment of the coated samples was performed in a laboratory incubator (CLIMACELL incubator, MMM Group, Munich, Germany). The samples were tested under different temperatures, including exposure to 100 °C in a laboratory incubator and −15 °C in a cooling chamber, for durations of 1, 4, 9, 16 and 50 h. The maintenance of the hydrophobic characteristics was evaluated through contact angle measurements. The behavior of the hydrophobic nickel foams was also examined in both acidic and alkaline environments. Specifically, the samples were immersed in hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solutions for 1, 4, 6, 9, 16 and 50 h. Contact angles were measured after each period to evaluate the retention of the hydrophobic properties of the samples.

The viscosity of oils used in the experiments was measured with a rheometer (TA Discovery Hybrid Rheometer HR30). Separation efficiency was calculated by dividing the weight of oil collected after filtration (m1) by the initial weight of oil before separation (mo), as described by Equation (1).
(1)
Separation efficiency =
m
1
m
o
* 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration of AgNO3 solution and immersion time
ce:section.ce:para: The development of micro-roughness on the nickel foam substrate is achieved through immersion in a silver nitrate (AgNO₃) solution, a crucial step in the creation of a hydrophobic surface. In order to test its effectiveness, silver nitrate solutions of varying concentrations (4 mM, 7 mM, and 20 mM) were used, and residence times of 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min were examined. The effect of varying silver nitrate concentrations on hydrophobicity was evaluated by measuring the water contact angles. Each contact angle measurement was repeated five times. The highest contact angle of 145° was measured when the solution concentration was 20 mM and the immersion time was 20 min. The outer deposited layers formed weaker bonds, particularly between 20 and 30 min of immersion, leading to easy detachment. This was confirmed by the WCA measurements, where superficial detachment of the outer layers was observed in the water droplets, indicating reduced surface stability and weaker layer cohesion ( Fig. 2a).

The determination of the optimal residence time in the silver nitrate solution was further investigated through UV measurements. Fig. 2b shows a typical absorption spectrum from 16 measurements, with a peak around 304 nm that corresponds to nitrate anions [38]. The strongest absorption is influenced by interactions with the solvent (ethanol), making it challenging to directly assess variations in Ag+ concentration. To monitor nitrate ion absorption, samples were collected at regular intervals over a 30-minute period and the resulting trend is shown in Fig. 2c. Each absorption value corresponds to the average of five measurements. Fig. 2d shows the corresponding concentration changes, calculated based on the Beer-Lambert law (Equation 2) [39].

The molecular absorption coefficient (5.7 M⁻¹cm⁻¹) was measured from the first sample, where both nitrate ion concentration and absorption were known.
(2)
A = αλ ∗ b ∗ c
where A is the absorbance, αλ represents the absorptivity of the substance that reduces the light intensity passing through the sample, b is the optical path length in cm, c is the concentration of the substance that absorbs light.

It is observed that, at 20 min, the concentration of nitrate ions increases. Due to the preservation of the solution volume and stoichiometry, the optimal silver deposition time was calculated to be 20 min of immersion in the silver nitrate solution, corresponding to the maximum absorption and concentration of nitrate ions. The determination of the optimal immersion time in the stearic acid solution was examined through contact angle measurements at various time intervals and the highest contact angle of 147° was measured for an immersion time of 50 min (Fig. 2e).

Fig. 2f illustrates the intrinsic hydrophilicity of the pristine nickel foam substrate, as evidenced by its complete immersion in water. Following surface modification, the substrate exhibits pronounced hydrophobicity, remaining buoyant at the air–water interface and thereby demonstrating the successful development of water-repellent characteristics. Fig. 1g further emphasizes this transformation by directly comparing the untreated foam with the modified one, whose enhanced reflectivity arises from the deposited silver layer.

High-magnification SEM analysis of the hydrophobic nickel foam under optimized modification conditions ( Fig. 3) reveals the coexistence of two distinct surface morphologies. The unmodified nickel foam structure is presented in Fig. 3a for comparison. Following sequential immersion in silver nitrate and stearic acid solutions, Fig. 3(b–d) show the formation of (i) uniformly distributed silver particles, likely originating from the in situ reduction of silver ions on the foam substrate and subsequent nanoparticle agglomeration, and (ii) larger flake-like surface features, which are plausibly attributed to the formation of a silver stearate phase during the stearic acid treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and chemical stability of the hydrophobic nickel foams
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 evaluates the stability of the hydrophobic nickel foam coatings under different environmental conditions using water contact angle (WCA) measurements as a function of residence time (0–50 h). In Fig. 4a, the foams were subjected to thermal stress at –15 °C (cooling chamber) and 100 °C (laboratory incubator). The WCA decreased from ∼146° to ∼135° at −15 °C, whereas at 100 °C it decreased to ∼140° after 50 h of exposure, indicating gradual loss of hydrophobicity under prolonged temperature exposure, though the surfaces remained strongly water-repellent throughout. Fig. 4b examines the chemical stability of the coatings in acidic (HCl) and alkaline (NaOH) solutions. An initial drop in WCA is observed within the first few hours, after which the values stabilize near 135°, demonstrating the persistence of hydrophobic properties despite corrosive environments.

Fig. 4c assesses the foams in 3.5 % NaCl solution (simulating seawater) and in distilled water as a control. Both media produce a similar trend: a moderate decline in WCA during the first few hours, followed by stabilization around 135°, confirming that the coatings retain their hydrophobicity even in saline conditions. The slight reduction in water contact angle (WCA) observed in the 3.5 % NaCl solution (∼135°) can be attributed to time-dependent interactions between chloride ions (Cl⁻) and the stearate layer, which may lead to partial disruption or ion exchange at the organic–metal interface. Over prolonged exposure, these interactions can induce minor localized corrosion beneath the coating, slightly altering the surface chemistry and microstructure. Initially, however, the coating exhibited excellent protective behavior, as confirmed by the electrochemical measurements and the corresponding Bode plots (shown in Section 3.4), demonstrating strong barrier properties. Despite the marginal decrease in WCA, the surface remains highly hydrophobic, indicating that the coating continues to provide effective protection and maintains its functional stability under saline conditions.

Images of the coated Ni foams after 50 h of exposure are shown in Fig. 4d, along with the corresponding image of the initial coated foam prior to exposure for comparison. No visible alteration was observed upon visual inspection, as the coated nickel foam remained shiny and metallic in appearance after exposure, indicating the absence of oxidation, corrosion, or surface dulling, and retaining a uniform surface morphology. The lack of discoloration, peeling, or noticeable surface degradation further demonstrates that the modified nickel foam preserves its structural integrity and hydrophobic performance even after prolonged immersion in saline solution, as further confirmed by the WCA results.

Overall, the results across all panels demonstrate that the hydrophobic nickel foams exhibit excellent thermal and chemical stability against acidic, alkaline, and saline stresses. Even after 50 h of exposure, the WCA consistently remains above 135°, attesting their potential for practical applications in harsh environments. Contact angles measured for the study of the hydrophobic coating’s stability were found to be equal to or even higher than the values reported in the literature [40–46], for the corresponding exposure times. It is worth noting that, in many studies, the contact angle is determined using a droplet of the respective solution employed for the chemical stability tests. In contrast, in the present work the samples were first immersed in the solutions for specified residence times, so that the entire surface was exposed to the corrosive environment, and subsequently the water contact angle was measured. This methodological approach provides a more representative evaluation of the stability and durability of the hydrophobic coating, as it captures the actual effect of the environment on the surface wettability.

Oil-water separation experiments were conducted using a small-scale separation tank designed for this purpose ( Fig. 5a) at 25 °C with a volume ratio of Voil: Vwater = 1:3. Three types of oils with varying viscosities were tested: sunflower oil (low viscosity), engine oils 10W-40 (medium viscosity) and 80W-90 (high viscosity) (Fig. 5b). The separation efficiency of the superhydrophobic copper foams was found to be influenced by the viscosity of the oil. As shown in Fig. 5b, the three oils tested—sunflower oil, 10W–40 engine oil, and 80W–90 engine oil—presented markedly different viscosities, ranging from approximately 0.05 Pa·s for sunflower oil to nearly 0.25 Pa·s for the most viscous 80W–90 oil. This difference in viscosity was directly reflected in the separation performance (Fig. 5c). Oils with lower viscosity, such as sunflower oil, were separated with slightly higher efficiency, approaching 99 %. In contrast, the more viscous 80W–90 engine oil showed a modest reduction in efficiency, though still above 95 %. The intermediate-viscosity 10W–40 oil exhibited performance between these two extremes. Importantly, all hydrophobic nickel foams maintained excellent and stable separation efficiency—above 95 %—even after 15 consecutive filtration cycles, demonstrating their robustness and reusability regardless of oil type. During each filtration cycle, 20 ml of oil was filtered. In general, the separation efficiency values observed in this study are comparable to, or even higher than, those reported in similar studies in the literature [41,47–49].

Several studies have reported oil-water separation using superhydrophobic and superoleophilic nickel foams with varying efficiencies. The separation efficiency values reported in the literature for nickel foams are generally comparable to those achieved in the present work. However, the present method offers clear advantages in terms of simplicity and cost-effectiveness. Unlike the reported methods, which involve multiple, intricate, and often expensive steps requiring precise control and advanced techniques, this approach is straightforward, economical, and easily reproducible, while still maintaining high separation performance in oil-water applications. Table 1 shows the separation efficiency of various modified nickel foams, differing in materials and modification methods. It also allows a straightforward comparison with the present approach in terms of performance, simplicity, and cost. Specifically, octyl graphene oxide-modified nickel foam achieved over 97.6 % separation efficiency for benzene/water, while SiO₂-PNF/NiCo nickel foam reached 99 % for n-hexane/water. Cerium-based nickel foam exhibited separation efficiency greater than 96 %, and PTFE-coated nickel foam showed values ranging from 96 % to 99 % [50–53].

Fig. 6a shows a hydrophobic nickel foam, immersed in a beaker containing water and engine oil droplets. The hydrophobic/oleophilic nature of the foam, allows it to absorb the oil effectively. To assess the effectiveness of water-oil separation using hydrophobic nickel foams, as well as to investigate how repeated filtration cycles influence the overall separation performance, samples shaped in a “U” configuration were used. to facilitate the efficient accumulation of oil within their structure. Each sample underwent a total of eleven filtration cycles, using a peristaltic pump, during which the time required to absorb a specified volume of oil was recorded. During each cycle, 20 ml of oil was filtered. Fig. 6(b,c) depict the peristaltic pump employed to induce forced flow in the water-oil separation setup and the separation process, respectively. The mass flow rate, defined as the amount of oil absorbed per unit time and was calculated for each filtration cycle to evaluate whether the required separation time decreased as the number of filtrations increased.".

The mass flow rate was calculated using the following Eq. 3:
(3)
m ̇ =
m
oil
t

The mass flow rate was maintained consistently with minimal variation across eleven filtration cycles, demonstrating both the effectiveness and durability of the hydrophobic foam. This stability suggests that the material retains its structural integrity and separation efficiency over repeated use (Fig. 6d). The separation efficiency of the hydrophobic nickel foams was also evaluated, remaining at 97 % after 10 filtration cycles.

The main goal of developing the superhydrophobic nickel foam was to achieve highly efficient and durable oil–water separation through a simple and cost-effective fabrication method. The fabricated foam exhibited excellent separation performance, maintaining high efficiency over at least 15 repeated cycles, which confirms the robustness and stability of the hydrophobic coating.

The immersion-based modification using silver nitrate and stearic acid is advantageous for practical applications due to its simplicity, low cost, and minimal chemical consumption. Both reagents are used in small quantities, and the process does not require complex instrumentation, making it environmentally and economically attractive for scale-up.

In terms of scalability, the method can be extended to larger surfaces or complex geometries by employing continuous or batch immersion processes in larger tanks, ensuring uniform deposition of the hierarchical micro/nano structures that are essential for achieving superhydrophobicity. Process parameters such as immersion time, reagent concentration, and agitation could be optimized to maintain consistent coating quality across non-flat or porous surfaces.

From an industrial perspective, the developed nickel foam shows great promise for large-scale oil–water separation and filtration processes, including applications such as treatment of oily wastewater or oil spill remediation. The foam’s high porosity, chemical stability, and reusability make it suitable for integration into filtration modules, gravity-driven separators, and continuous flow systems. Future work could focus on continuous coating systems, roll-to-roll immersion setups, or electrochemical assistance to further improve coating uniformity on complex geometries.

Overall, these strategies demonstrate that the proposed fabrication approach is both practical and scalable for real-world separation and filtration technologies while maintaining high efficiency and durability

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to study the corrosion behavior of hydrophobic nickel foam. Uncoated nickel foam was also tested for comparison. All measurements were carried out at room temperature (25 °C) using a three-electrode setup with a 3.5 wt% sodium chloride (NaCl) solution as the electrolyte. Before starting the EIS measurements, the samples were placed in the solution for at least 30 min at open circuit potential (OCP) to allow the system to stabilize. The impedance data were collected at OCP over a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz, using a small alternating voltage of 10 mV. Each experiment was done in an open container with 50 ml of solution, exposed to air, under the same environmental conditions.

Because metallic foams have a complex, porous structure, it is challenging to measure their surface area. The high porosity and irregular geometry make it difficult to accurately determine the surface area in contact with the solution, which affects the precision of calculations. Hydrophobic coatings add more difficulty, since they often cover the surface unevenly. To achieve comparable results, a value of 2.2 cm² was used, which matches the footprint of the foam. Each sample was immersed halfway into the solution so that only half of its total surface was in contact with the electrolyte. All samples had the same size and shape, ensuring equal exposure in every test. This approach helps reduce errors due to the foam’s complex structure and gives more reliable corrosion data from the EIS measurements.

Fig. 7 presents the Nyquist and Bode plots for both uncoated and hydrophobic nickel foam. Both plots for the hydrophobic foam revealed a distinct diffusion-controlled behavior. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 7a) exhibited a straight line at approximately 45° to the real axis, indicating the presence of Warburg impedance and suggesting that the corrosion process is limited by mass transport of reactive species to the electrode surface. It indicates that mass transport (diffusion of species to or from the electrode surface) is limiting the corrosion process. The foam’s hydrophobic nature limits the transport of corrosive species to the metal surface. This indicates that the foam acts as a barrier, limiting the transport of corrosive species and thereby slowing down the corrosion process. The absence of a clear semicircle suggests very high charge-transfer resistance, i.e., the metal is effectively shielded. Hydrophobic foam likely creates air pockets or micro-barriers, which increase diffusion path lengths.

Correspondingly, the Bode magnitude plot Fig. 7b showed a frequency-dependent increase in impedance at lower frequencies, with the slope in the log|Z| vs. log(f) representation approaching –0.5, consistent with diffusion-controlled kinetics. At high frequencies, the impedance was dominated by the solution resistance, remaining relatively constant. These results indicate that the hydrophobic foam effectively impedes the transport of corrosive species, thereby acting as a barrier and mitigating the corrosion rate.

For the uncoated foam, the Nyquist plot exhibited a nearly perfect semicircular profile, characteristic of a corrosion process dominated by charge-transfer resistance. This was corroborated by the Bode magnitude plot, which displayed a distinct plateau at low frequencies corresponding to a well-defined DC resistance, indicating that the corrosion reaction reached a steady state under kinetic control.

These findings demonstrate that the hydrophobic coating effectively impedes the transport of corrosive species to the substrate, altering the corrosion mechanism from charge-transfer–controlled to diffusion-controlled. Overall, the results indicate that the hydrophobic coating significantly contributes to the protection and long-term durability of the nickel foam. The experimental data are shown as points, and the fitted curves are shown as lines.

The produced hydrophobic nickel foams exhibited a dense coverage of silver nanoparticles and flake-like structures formed through stearic acid interactions. They achieved a water contact angle (WCA) of 147° and maintained values above 135° after 50 h of exposure to various harsh conditions, confirming their robust thermal and chemical stability. They also demonstrated separation efficiencies above 95 % over 15 cycles, showing excellent reusability. Additionally, the hydrophobic coating significantly enhanced corrosion resistance in NaCl solution, highlighting its protective capability. These findings demonstrate that the produced superhydrophobic nickel foams combine robust stability, high efficiency in oil–water separation, and effective corrosion resistance, making them promising candidates for practical applications in environmental remediation and chemical processes.

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. The publication of this article in Open Access mode was financially supported by HEAL-Link.

Fani Stergioudi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Aikaterini Baxevani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Eleni Lamprou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental impacts and challenges associated with oil spills on shorelines
J. Mar. Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Asif, Z., Chen, Z., An, C., & Dong, J. (2022). Environmental Impacts and Challenges Associated with Oil Spills on Shorelines. Journal of Marine Science and Engineering, 10(6), 762. https://doi.org/10.3390/jmse10060762
